title: Tirzah: Minimal as a Heartbeat
author: Leelou Blanc
body:
A Tirzah song at its most stripped down sounds like three elements held loosely together, her voice, a beat that barely arrives, and silence. The silence is doing something.

Tirzah Mastin is from Essex, England. She's been making music with producer Mica Levi for over a decade, and their collaboration defines her sound completely. Levi writes and produces everything. Tirzah sings over it. The result doesn't sound like either of them would make separately.

[spotify:artist/6f5lOlSFJw9K79gaNnmWAd]

*Devotion* (2018) was the first time most people heard her outside of small circles. Songs like "Devotion" and "Fine Again" used R&B as a starting point and then subtracted everything that made it recognizable. What's left is something skeletal and genuinely affecting. The production on those tracks sits so far back that the voice floats in near-empty space, and that emptiness is not absence. It is pressure. The restraint creates a tension that conventional production, with its layers and fills and movement, cannot replicate.

## Colourgrade and After

*Colourgrade* (2021) pushed further. There are moments on that record where the production almost collapses under its own weight, bass that flickers in and out, beats that stutter and restart, melodies that arrive late. It shouldn't work. It does. The album earned strong critical attention precisely because it operated so confidently in territory that most producers avoid, the space where a track might dissolve but holds, where the rhythm section is present but tenuous, where everything sounds like it was made at the exact threshold of coherence.

The 2023 EP *trip9love...???* is even more abstracted. If *Devotion* was Tirzah finding her voice, *trip9love* is her testing whether voice is necessary at all. It's a short, strange record that rewards patience. The title itself, with its ellipsis and question marks, signals an approach to form that treats uncertainty as a structural element rather than something to be resolved.

## Mica Levi's Role

Levi's production work outside this collaboration, the score for Under the Skin, the orchestral pieces, the film work, operates on similar principles. Levi understands that unease can be beautiful, that the wrong note held long enough becomes the right note, that production is not about filling space but about shaping it. What makes the Tirzah collaboration distinctive is how completely Levi's instincts and Tirzah's vocal character merge. There is no sense that the voice is being placed over a beat. There is a sense that voice and beat are a single organism with two components.

The fact that Levi writes and produces everything while Tirzah performs everything means the collaboration has a clarity of roles that prevents compromise. Neither party is adjusting to the other's preferences. They are building a shared language, and that language has been developing across multiple records into something increasingly coherent and increasingly strange.

## What she's building

What she's building is a body of work that defies easy categorization. It's too abstract to be R&B, too rooted in emotion to be purely experimental. "Do You Know" from *Colourgrade* is probably the closest she gets to accessible, and it's still deeply strange, the rhythm section feels like it's tracking something other than a standard grid. The hook is present but the arrangement around it refuses to behave, shifting and stuttering in ways that conventional pop production would smooth out.

She doesn't do many interviews. She rarely performs. That distance from the promotional machinery feels intentional, and it fits the music. Mica Levi's production is similarly non-promotional, no radio-friendly mixes, no attempts to make the work go bigger than it wants to go.

## The Question of Emotion

What makes Tirzah's minimalism affecting rather than merely conceptually interesting is that the emotional content of the songs is not reduced by the stripped-back production. It is concentrated. When there are fewer sounds, each sound carries more weight. A single bass note holds grief that a conventional production might disperse across twenty tracks. A vocal line with nothing underneath it except sparse percussion becomes impossible to look away from.

Most minimalist music in the experimental tradition treats emotion with suspicion, as if raw feeling were a contamination of the conceptual clarity being pursued. Tirzah's work treats emotion as the point. The minimalism is in service of the feeling, not a retreat from it. That inversion explains why her music reaches people who have no investment in experimental music as a category. They encounter it through the emotion first and discover the structure second.

The temptation for any producer working in this mode is to add things, to fill silences that feel uncomfortable, to provide the listener with more conventional signposts. Levi consistently refuses this temptation. The silences on a Tirzah record are not mistakes or omissions. They are decisions, and they are right.

## The Entry Points

"Fine Again" is the easiest starting point. The vocal is more conventional there, the production still restrained but not yet at the level of abstraction that the later work reaches. It is accessible without being simple. "Do You Know" is the track that stays with you longest, the one that has the most conventional melodic logic while still carrying Tirzah's characteristic sense of dislocation. "trip9love" the song is where you go once you need more, once the earlier work has calibrated your ears to what she is doing and you are ready for her to push that further.

There's a stubbornness to what she does that's increasingly rare. Making music this deliberately uncommercial, at this level of craft, without apology, represents a commitment to a specific vision that the industry does not reward and cannot manufacture. The audience for it is real and growing. That growth is happening on the music's terms, not anyone else's.

She and Levi have been working together since they were teenagers in the same music program. That duration of collaboration produces a shared understanding that no amount of professional rapport can replicate. They know what they are trying to do. They know how to tell when they have done it. The records they have made together are the evidence of that knowledge, accumulated across a decade of work that has never tried to be anything other than exactly what it is.
